subject: ap-chemistry
theme: Thermodynamics governs whether reactions are possible, while electrochemistry harnesses electron transfer between half-reactions to produce or consume electrical energy.

core_concepts.title: Entropy and the Second Law
core_concepts.detail: Entropy ($S$) is a measure of disorder or the number of microstates available to a system. Gases > liquids > solids in entropy. Reactions that increase moles of gas or disorder increase entropy ($\Delta S > 0$). The second law states that the total entropy of the universe increases in all spontaneous processes: $\Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{surr} > 0$.

core_concepts.title: Gibbs Free Energy
core_concepts.detail: $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ at constant $T$ and $P$. $\Delta G < 0$: spontaneous (exergonic). $\Delta G > 0$: non-spontaneous (endergonic). $\Delta G = 0$: at equilibrium. When $\Delta H < 0$ and $\Delta S > 0$: always spontaneous. When $\Delta H > 0$ and $\Delta S < 0$: never spontaneous. Otherwise, temperature determines spontaneity.

core_concepts.title: $\Delta G^\circ$ and Equilibrium
core_concepts.detail: $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$. If $K > 1$: $\Delta G^\circ < 0$ (products favored). If $K < 1$: $\Delta G^\circ > 0$ (reactants favored). $\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT\ln Q$; at equilibrium ($Q = K$), $\Delta G = 0$. Also: $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ_{cell}$ (connects thermodynamics to electrochemistry).

core_concepts.title: Galvanic (Voltaic) Cells
core_concepts.detail: Galvanic cells convert chemical energy to electrical energy via spontaneous redox reactions. Oxidation occurs at the anode (negative terminal in galvanic cell); reduction at the cathode (positive terminal). Electrons flow through the external circuit from anode to cathode. A salt bridge maintains electrical neutrality by allowing ion migration between half-cells.

core_concepts.title: Standard Cell Potentials
core_concepts.detail: $E^\circ_{cell} = E^\circ_{cathode} - E^\circ_{anode}$ (using standard reduction potentials). Positive $E^\circ_{cell}$ means the reaction is spontaneous under standard conditions. The species with the higher (more positive) reduction potential is reduced; the one with the lower potential is oxidized.

core_concepts.title: Electrolytic Cells and Faraday's Law
core_concepts.detail: Electrolytic cells use external electrical energy to drive non-spontaneous reactions ($E^\circ_{cell} < 0$, $\Delta G^\circ > 0$). Faraday's Law: $m = MIt/(nF)$ calculates the mass deposited/dissolved. $F = 96,485$ C/mol electrons. In electroplating, the object to be plated is the cathode (reduction).

core_concepts.title: The Nernst Equation
core_concepts.detail: At non-standard conditions: $E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q = E^\circ - \frac{0.0592}{n}\log Q$ (at $25^\circ C$). When Q < 1 (reactants favored), $E > E^\circ$. When Q > 1 (products favored), $E < E^\circ$. At equilibrium ($Q = K$, $E = 0$): $E^\circ = (0.0592/n) \log K$.

formulas.name: Gibbs free energy
formulas.description: Combines enthalpy and entropy to determine spontaneity at constant temperature and pressure; negative $\Delta G$ means the process is thermodynamically spontaneous.

formulas.name: $\Delta G^\circ$ and equilibrium constant
formulas.description: Relates the standard Gibbs free energy change to the equilibrium constant; $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K, $T$ in Kelvin.

formulas.name: Standard cell potential to $\Delta G^\circ$
formulas.description: Connects thermodynamics and electrochemistry; $n$ = moles of electrons transferred, $F = 96,485$ C/mol, $E^\circ_{cell}$ in volts.
formulas.example: For a Zn/Cu cell with $E^\circ = 1.10$ V and $n = 2$: $\Delta G^\circ = -(2)(96485)(1.10) = -212,000$ J $= -212$ kJ.

formulas.name: Nernst equation (at $25^\circ C$)
formulas.description: Calculates cell potential under non-standard conditions at $25^\circ C$; $n$ is moles of electrons transferred, $Q$ is the reaction quotient.

formulas.name: Faraday's law
formulas.description: Mass ($m$ in grams) deposited at an electrode; $M$ = molar mass (g/mol), $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $n$ = electrons per ion, $F = 96,485$ C/mol.

exam_tip: The four-way connection in electrochemistry is critical: $E^\circ_{cell}$, $\Delta G^\circ$, $K$, and spontaneity are all linked. Positive $E^\circ_{cell}$ $\Leftrightarrow$ negative $\Delta G^\circ$ $\Leftrightarrow$ $K > 1$ $\Leftrightarrow$ spontaneous. Memorize this chain. For the Nernst equation, remember: $Q < 1$ means more reactant than product $\Rightarrow$ $E > E^\circ$; $Q > 1$ means more product $\Rightarrow$ $E < E^\circ$.
